text: During a laparoscopic left colectomy, the surgeon performs an intraoperative colonoscopy to identify and mark the position of a lesion in the descending colon of a six to eight-year-old male patient. The procedure involves complete mesocolic excision while preserving the inferior mesenteric vein.

During a laparoscopic left colectomy, the surgeon performs an intraoperative colonoscopy to identify and mark the position of a lesion in the descending colon. The procedure involves a complete mesocolic excision while preserving the inferior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection between the inferior mesenteric artery and vein, detaching the descending mesocolon from the retroperitoneum. The origin of the inferior mesenteric artery is identified, and surrounding fat and lymph nodes are removed. The inferior mesenteric vein is used as a landmark to detach it from the descending mesocolon, preserving it to facilitate venous drainage. Branches entering the descending mesocolon are ligated, preparing for a higher anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection between the artery and vein, detaching the descending mesocolon from the retroperitoneum. The origin of the inferior mesenteric artery is identified, and surrounding fat and nodes are removed. The inferior mesenteric vein is used as a landmark for further dissection, detaching it from the descending mesocolon while preserving it to facilitate venous drainage. Branches entering the descending mesocolon are ligated to enable a higher anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection and creates a window to access the lesser sac. The preserved vein is visible below the pancreas as the dissection continues medially to laterally above the pancreas, detaching the descending and transverse mesocolon from the pancreas. The coloepiploic ligaments are detached from the distal transverse mesocolon, and the splenic flexure is separated from above. The lateral mobilization is completed, fully detaching the left colon.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection and creates a window to access the lesser sac. The preserved vein is visible below the pancreas as the dissection continues medially to laterally above the pancreas. The descending and transverse mesocolon are detached from the pancreas, and the coloepiploic ligaments are separated from the distal transverse mesocolon. The splenic flexure is detached from above, completing the lateral mobilization of the left colon.

The surgeon selects the point for distal transection of the colon, ensuring it is at least ten centimeters from the tumor. A similar point is chosen on the transverse mesocolon, ten centimeters proximal to the tumor. The marginal arcade is detached on both sides, and the colon is transected using a stapler. The specimen remains in place as a side-to-side anastomosis is performed. The hole is closed with a first layer of barbed suture, followed by a second layer of running suture to reinforce it. The procedure concludes with a check of the vein's integrity and drainage, and the surgical specimen is displayed.

The surgeon selects the point for distal transection of the colon, ensuring it is at least ten centimeters from the tumor. Similarly, the transverse mesocolon is transected ten centimeters proximal to the tumor. The marginal arcade is detached on both sides, and the colon is transected using a stapler. The specimen is left in place, and a side-to-side anastomosis is performed.

The surgeon closes the surgical site with a first layer of barbed suture, followed by a second layer of running suture to reinforce the closure. The integrity of the preserved vein and its drainage is checked. The surgical specimen is displayed.
